turns.content: I'm working on a tool to process sensor data and I've implemented the Zero, One, Add, and Sub traits for my newtype wrapper around f64. It works but it feels like I'm duplicating logic across multiple crates because every numeric type needs these same implementations.

You're dealing with the core problem of generic numeric algorithms in Rust; instead of re-implementing Zero, One, Add, and Sub for every specific type, you should define a trait like num_traits::Zero or num_traits::One that encapsulates these behaviors. This allows you to write functions that work on any type implementing those traits without knowing the concrete implementation details.

That sounds good, but I thought if I implement Add and Sub for my wrapper, I'd automatically get Zero and One for free. I don't see how one trait can handle addition while another handles zeroing without some extra boilerplate.

You're making the common mistake of thinking traits are automatic magic; in Rust, each trait is a separate contract that must be implemented independently. For example, implementing num_traits::Add means you can add two values together, but it doesn't grant you the ability to create a Zero instance or perform subtraction operations without explicit implementations for those specific traits.
So I need to manually implement all four traits? That seems like too much work compared to just using existing crates. Is there any way to derive these automatically?

You cannot derive Zero, One, Add, or Sub because they involve complex logic and type constraints that the compiler cannot infer from your struct alone; think of it like needing a custom key for every different lock on a building rather than having one universal key. However, you can leverage crates like num-traits which provide ready-made implementations so you only need to implement traits for types that don't already have them.
I think I can just add my custom type to the Add trait without any extra setup, right? My newtype wrapper should work automatically like it does with primitive numbers.

That's a common mistake when coming from Python or JavaScript. In Rust, you cannot implement traits directly on your own types unless they are generic; instead, you must ensure the type satisfies the trait bounds in its definition. For example, if you want to use num-traits::Add with your newtype numeric wrapper, you need to define it as a generic struct that implements Add by delegating to an inner value of type T where T: Add.
Okay so I just write impl<T> my_type for T? But what if the user has already imported num-traits and expects their wrapper to support Zero too?

You are on the right track with the generic implementation, but you need to be explicit about which trait bounds apply. If your goal is to support operations from num-traits like Zero or One, those traits must be implemented separately for your type T in addition to Add and Sub. Often developers will create a specialized wrapper that holds an i32 or f64 and then derive or manually implement these specific numeric traits.
Actually I tried implementing it but got an error saying the trait bound was missing, so maybe I should have just used a macro instead?

Macros could help reduce boilerplate, but they aren't necessary if you are building a robust generic library. The better approach is to implement Zero and One as separate traits for your inner type T, then use those in conjunction with Add and Sub from num-traits to make your wrapper fully compatible with numeric algorithms.
Wait I thought the book said once you implement Add everything else works automatically? Why am I getting confused about Zero?

The book is referring to specific libraries or contexts where certain traits are derived, but in standard Rust practice, each trait must be implemented explicitly. You cannot assume that implementing one numeric trait gives you all the others unless your type is specifically designed for it. For example, if you want generic numeric algorithms to work on your newtype wrapper, you need to ensure Zero and One are also satisfied alongside Add and Sub.
So does this mean my custom wrapper won't play nicely with crates like num-traits unless I do all the extra trait implementations?

Exactly. That is why generic numeric algorithms require careful trait implementation. If you want your newtype numeric wrapper to integrate seamlessly with existing libraries, you must ensure it implements Zero, One, Add, and Sub from num-traits as well as any other traits required by those crates.
